= 0.54), I is integrated fluorescence emission intensity, A is UV absorbance at excitation wavelength which gives the highest fluorescence intensity called optical density, and η is reflective index of CDs and quinine sulfate reference (Ref).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biomass and lignin-derived CDs and their characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: Lignocellulose biomass is found in the cell walls of plants and it is primarily composed of carbohydrate homo-polymers (cellulose, hemicellulose), and an aromatic hetero-polymer (lignin). The by-products from agro-industries are the main sources of lignocellulose such as lignin, cellulose and hemicellulose from the pulp and paper industry, cassava pulp from starch industry, and sugarcane bagasse from sugar industry, etc. Cellulose is a polysaccharide consisting of a linear chain of several hundreds to thousands of ß-1,4-glycosidic linkages to forming a long line of d-glucose units. Hemicellulose found in lignocellulose is hetero-polymers with xylan backbone and several branched molecules such as glucuronic acid (glucuronoxylan), arabinose (arabinoxylan), mannose (glucomannan), acetic acid (acetylated xylan) and xyloglucan. These polysaccharides contain different sugar monomers. In contrast to hemicellulose, cellulose contains solely anhydrous glucose subunits. The branching structure of hemicellulose therefore makes it easier to degrade into small molecules and serves as a precursor for the bottom-up synthesis of CDs. Lignin has often been regarded as a byproduct in the process of breaking down plant cell walls to extract polysaccharides mainly cellulose and hemicellulose, which can then be converted into fuels or other useful products through hydrolysis and fermentation. Due to its complexity and heterogeneity, lignin utilization and valorization to biofuels such as pyrolysis oils, biobased materials such as lignin nanoparticles [47], lignin-based polyurethane [48], polystyrene [49], lignin-based film [50], as well as biobased chemicals such as phenolic compounds [51–53], and mediators or electrolytes [54] have been less developed compared with cellulose and hemicellulose. As the worldwide endeavor to find optimal lignocellulosic materials for biofuel generation progresses, the significance of comprehending the structure, makeup, and amount of lignin present in plant cell walls has become crucial.

Many research works studied on carbon-based nanomaterial synthesis from lignocellulosic biomass. Chai et al. [55] synthesized GQDs, spongy carbon, and sugars from sugarcane bagasse under hydrothermal carbonization technique at a temperature between 140 and 180 °C for 4 h. After centrifugal and dialysis using a membrane (MWCO of 500 Da) against ultrapure water, the size of GQDs was studied using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) showing 2.26 nm in diameter and the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm assigned to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphitic carbon [56], while the atomic force electron microscopy (AFM) displayed the height of GQDs at around 5 nm. The GQD aqueous solution appeared faint yellow under sunlight and glowed bright blue under UV light at 365 nm. The GQDs showed a UV–vis absorption peak at 280 nm with a weaker shoulder peak around 320 nm, corresponding to the π–π* transitions of the CC bond and the n-π* transition of the CO bond, respectively. The maximumFL emission at 461 nm was obtained at the excitation wavelength (λEx) of 390 nm. Additionally, the "giant red-edge effect" was observed, indicating that the emission wavelength (λEm) was highly dependent on the excitation wavelength. This was attributed to the graphene structure and high oxygen content of GQDs that slowed down the fluorescence phenomenon of GQDs in polar solvent [57]. Thus, the emission peaks λEm were red-shifted from 461 nm to 510 nm when the λEx was altered from 390 nm to 440 nm. The obtained GQDs exhibited excellent and stable optical properties, making them promising for applications in sensors, biological imaging, and other areas. In another work, CDs derived from palm empty fruit bunch were synthesized using one-pot hydrothermal synthesis with polyethylene glycol (CDs-PEG) at 220 °C for 6 h for bioimaging and colon cancer therapy applications [58]. The green emissive CDs-PEG with a maximum λEm of 400 nm was obtained at λEx = 240 nm. The HR-TEM image analysis of CDs-PEG revealed that the average core carbon size of CDs-PEG was 4.50 nm (lattice spacing = 0.306 nm), which is comparable to that of CDs synthesized at 220 °C for 6 h (4.47 nm with d-spacing of 0.395 nm). CDs synthesized at 220 °C for 6 and 10 h displayed an interference-free background in the mass range of m/z 25–850, indicating their suitability for analyzing small molecules using MALDI-TOF-MS [59]. In the case of CDs-PEG, cationic molecules were detected in the m/z range of 1000–2000, confirming the presence of PEG on the CD surface. The conjugation of doxorubicin (DOX), an anticancer drug, onto CDs-PEG particles, was observed through the MALDI-TOF-MS peaks at m/z 396 and 399 in negative-ion mode. An enhanced QY and the red-shift emission of CDs produced from palm bunches from blue to yellowish-green emission were found when increasing the nitrogen (p-phenylenediamine) and sulfur (sodium thiosulfate) dopants concentrations [60]. The highest FL emission intensity, resulting from the principal number of particles being excited at their distinct excitation wavelengths was observed at λEx of 340 nm for CDs and 0.05 M NS/CDs. The λEx for maximum FL emission intensity was red-shifted to 400 nm for 0.30 M NS/CDs and 0.40 M NS/CDs. Similarly, a substantial shift in the maximum emission peak from λEm = 400 nm to 530 nm was detected. These pronounced red-shift occurrences attributed to N-and-S doping were due to either surface passivation by N and S, or the combination of N/S heteroatoms into the carbon assembly which decreases the optical bandgap of the CDs [60]. The presence of graphitic nitrogen, −CN, −CO, and −COOH groups on the CD surface influences the red-shifting FL emission of CDs attributed to the change of optoelectronic states [61].

Lignin is less utilized compared with cellulose and hemicellulose due to its structural complexity. It is predominantly imperative in the formation of plant cell walls, particularly in wood and bark as it provides rigidity and therefore makes wood hard to degrade. Chemical structure of lignin is a cross-linked phenolic polymer containing phenylpropanoid units [62] that can be classified into three major types of monomers; 1) sinapyl alcohol or syringyl unit (S), 2) coniferyl alcohol or guaiacyl unit (G), and 3) p-coumaryl alcohol or p-hydroxyphenol unit (H)) [63] as shown in Fig. 5 A. The utilization of lignin as a precursor for CD synthesis has been extensively investigated regarding the high carbon content and UV–vis absorbance ability of their aromatic structures that render it highly feasible to modify as biobased luminescent agents. Recent research revealed that lignin precursor required low-energy bottom-up synthesis and provided particularly tunable fluorescent efficiency and adjustable surface chemistry for targeting specific molecules [64–66].

Zhu et al. [25] studied the synthesis of CQDs from alkali lignin (AL) with N or S acid additives doping via acidolysis, followed by the hydrothermal process at 200 °C for 12 h. Fig. 5B demonstrated the average diameters of CQDs at 2.88 nm (blue; B-CQDs), 4.01 nm (cyan; C-CQDs), 4.56 nm (bluish green; G-CQDs), and 5.05 nm (yellowish green; Y-CQDs), when the dopants were N from 4-aminobenzoic acid, S from benzenesulfonic acid, N,S from 4-aminobenzene sulfonic acid, and N,S from 2,4-diaminobenzene sulfonic acid, respectively. Four multicolors of CQDs fluorescence were observed under UV light at 365 nm (inset). The optimum PL emission peaks in Fig. 5C exhibited a redshift phenomenon resulting in blue to yellowish green emissive color for B-CQDs, C-CQDs, G-CQDs, and Y-CQDs at the centered wavelength of λEm = 344, 360, 470, and 530 nm with λEx = 280, 280, 340, and 440 nm, respectively [25]. In accordance, combined N/S dopants at higher concentration toward CDs derived from palm biomass vis hydrothermal reaction could alter the carbon structure and their edges, and thus obviously shift the λEm from blue-emitting NS/CDs to yellowish green emitting NS/CDs which corresponds to the larger to narrower band gap energy, respectively [60]. Zhao et al. [34] successfully developed multicolor CQDs for light-emitting diode (LEDs) applications. CQDs were synthesized from o-phenylenediamine via a one-step solvothermal method using water and ethanol as solvents for easy purification and an environmentally friendly process. From Fig. 6 A, the different solvents and times of the CQDs synthesis process result in different carbon quantum size and their optical properties. The preparation of green and blue fluorescent CQDs (G-CQDs and B-CQDs, respectively) from alkali lignin was investigated via acidolysis and hydrothermal treatment. Fig. 6B shows Raman spectroscopic analysis of lignin-based CQDs. The disordered structure of carbon (or amorphous structure) is represented through D peak (ID) at 1350 and 1377 cm-1 which indicate the defects of sp2 carbon rings, and the graphitic carbon (or crystalline structure) of high G peak (IG) at 1583 and 1587 cm-1 [26] which is referred to the planar configuration of sp2 carbon. Higher integrated D and G bands of G-CQD exhibited more defects in carbon structure and more layer thickness contained in the structure, respectively, compared with B-CQDs.

Pure lignin-based carbon dots (pLCDs) were synthesized in γ-valerolactone (GVL)/water. Fig. 6C demonstrated UV–vis spectra of pLCDs at a wavelength of 320 nm which was the optimal excitation wavelength of PL measurement, while small shoulders around 224 and 278 nm represented the K band and R band, respectively as a result of n−Π* effect from unsaturated CO and CC bonds of LCDs structure. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra exhibited the functional groups of the alkali lignin (AL) raw material compared with pLCDs (Fig. 6D). The peaks of unsaturated CC bond vibration near the wavenumber of 1500 cm-1 and those of CO stretching of conjugated ketone/aromatic ring at ∼1700–1550 cm-1 were observed. The FTIR results of pLCDs suggested the fluorescent-related groups including mainly hydroxyl, carbonyl, and aromatic functional groups. Moreover, the intensity of pLCD peaks showed that AL was still retained after hydrothermal treatment but smaller size and molecular weight were obtained [27]. The characteristic FTIR peaks of lignin provided the transmittance peak of CC stretching vibration, aromatic conjugated CO, and benzene ring vibration after thermal degradation of lignin. The results corresponded to the structure of the lignin molecules after heating [67].

Lignosulfonate lignin was used as a carbon source in the microwave-irradiated synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) [68]. Different amounts of lignosulfonate lignin were mixed with Milli-Q water (25 mL), agitated until homogenous, and then ultrasonicated. The mixture was placed in a beaker and heated for 10 min in a household microwave oven. When the microwave reaction occured, the color of the mixture shifted to a deep brown. After cooling to room temperature, the product was mixed with Milli-Q water (25 mL) and then repeatedly sonicated. After centrifuging the solution to get rid of large particles, the brown supernatant was gathered and treated using a syringe filter. Then the filtered solution was again sonicated for complete dispersion. Finally, CDs were obtained and were dialyzed with ultrapure water. CDs powder was obtained from freeze-drying and kept at 4 °C. Next, reduced fluorescent CDs (r-FCD) were synthesized by mixing 0.1 g NaBH4 into the solution of CDs and stirred overnight. Fig. 7 depicts the whole r-FCD preparation process. The fluorescent properties of r-FCDs were shown under UV light (254 nm and 365 nm) with blue fluorescence. Different concentrations (50, 100, and 200 mg /25 mL) of lignin gave different production yields of 2.39%, 5.02%, and 4.18%, respectively. The functional group of r-FCDs was described using FT-IR spectra, and the OH, CH, CO, and COOH groups were discovered on the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of optical and physical properties of CDs by metal doping
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of CDs in combination with transition metals for biomedical applications has gained attention in recent years. CDs were conjugated with metallic nanoparticles, such as cobalt (Co) [69], copper (Cu) [70], lanthanum (La) [71], and platinum (Pt) [72], or bimetallic co-doping such as silver-copper (Ag/Cu) [73], platinum-ruthenium (Pt/Ru) [74], platinum-nickel (Pt/Ni) [75], etc., and used for various applications such as drug delivery for cancer therapy, wound healing, bioimaging, and biosensor. These metal/CD nanocomposite materials change the overall structure and electronic state. Thus, their catalytic properties can be improved due to their high reactivity from the electron/proton transfer mechanism among transition metals and CDs. Photo-responsive characteristics of N,S doped CDs conjugated with mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) on reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation to inactivate or kill gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria namely Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus, and Escherichia coli [76]. Besides, doping N/S onto the CD surface significantly decreased the CDs' bandgap energy and shifted the emission wavelength from blue to green. This narrower bandgap facilitates the more efficient nitrogen reactive species (NRS) generation from NS/CDs to inactivate the bacteria tested [77]. Further, platinum (Pt) is a noble metal that is known as one of the effective catalysts and enzyme-like characteristics in the industry. The capability to catalyze many chemical reactions including hydrogenation, oxidation, dehydrogenation, hydrogenolysis, carbonylation, and hydroxylation has been discovered. Pt acts as a peroxidase mimicking nanozyme which is non-protein material. Compared to natural peroxidases, peroxidase mimics showed more stability and were normally applied in biomedical applications, especially for in vitro diagnostics [78,79] since its surface is easily functionalized with other biomolecules. These provide a lot of utilization of platinum metal complexes as catalysts or catalyst precursors [80]. Moreover, the advantages of combined ruthenium-platinum (Ru-Pt) catalyst were described in a previous study in which both metals had a great potential for enhancement of catalyst activity [81]. Ru can dissociate hydrogen peroxide molecules, while Pt resists the excessive deactivation of catalyst. The formation of bimetallic Ru-Pt provides some specific properties; the crystalline particles are formed in the whole nanostructure, the Ru-Pt bimetallic clusters are stable as both oxidizing and reducing agents, and both metals act as excellent catalysts in many chemical reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of CDs and metal nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis methods of CDs on a large scale for commercialization and application purposes, key criteria that must be considered are inexpensive cost of production, waste production, and treatment, along with environmental friendliness and sustainability for the overall process. Considering the overall green manufacturing process, raw materials, energy catalysts, solvents, water, and air are taken into account while waste generation consists of liquid wastes, air/gaseous wastes, solid wastes, and energy loss [82]. Recently, green CDs synthesis has been proposed. In terms of raw materials, most of the natural carbon sources for green synthesis of CDs are categorized into three groups: 1) microorganisms such as edible mushroom (Pleurotus spp.) derived CDs [83], cyanobacteria derived CQDs [84], fermented yeast derived CNTs [84]; 2) plant-based resources such as CDs derived from cellulose [85], cotton [86], herb or fruit extracts [87], palm empty fruit bunch [58,88,89], sugars [90,91], lignin [64,68,92,93], CQDs from pine wood [94], CNTs derived from palm oil [95]; 3) animal-based resources such as CQDs from honey [96], egg yolk [97], CQDs and CDs derived from chicken features [98,99], and chitosan [100], etc. Apart from green and sustainable carbon precursors, environmentally friendly dopants and biobased solvents are important to enhance the PL properties of CDs. Nontoxic and high biocompatible dopants such as nitrogen (N), boron (B), phosphorus (P), and sulfur (S) in non-metal dopants [101–103] have been reported to enhance PL and QY characteristics, while metal dopants such as silver (Ag), gold (Au), platinum (Pt) play a vital role in enhanced catalytic activity at room temperature which is utmost required for application as electrocatalysts, antimicrobial and nanozymes [82].

Since the 1990s, the most commonly used methods for the controllable chemical synthesis of nanomaterials have involved a range of chemical reagents, some of which are toxic. The reducing agents used include NaBH4, N2H4, aldehydes such as formaldehyde, CO, ascorbic acid, hydroquinone, and citrate. Among these chemicals, NaBH is the most commonly used reducing agent such as the reduction of Ag⁺ ions to Ag° [104]. Capping agents have included PEI (polyethylene imine), PPI (polypropylene polybenzyl isocyanate), oleylamine, OA (oleic acid), linoleic acid, CTAB (cetrimonium bromide), PAMAM (poly(amidoamine)), PAA (poly(acrylic acid)), TOPO (trioctylphosphine oxide), and TOP (trioctylphosphine). In some cases, a reagent can serve as both a capping and reducing agent simultaneously. While these methodologies are frequently used, many involve toxic reagents and produce harmful residues [82]. The structure and functional groups of such dual-role agents affect the reduction process's kinetics, which in turn impacts the final properties of the material. Therefore, understanding on how to modulate this influence is crucial for achieving experimental control over the material's characteristics. Commonly used solvents in these processes include toluene, DMF (N,N-dimethylformamide), and others [105].

For the green process of carbon and metal nanoparticles, eco-friendly, and low toxic reducing agents derived from bioresources or biochemical production from microorganisms have been lately introduced such as ascorbic acid [106], citric acid [107], and α-amino acids [108] (e.g., l-Alanine (C3H7NO2), l-valine (C5H11NO2), l-isoleucine (C6H13NO2), DL-phenylalanine (C9H11NO2), etc) [109]. In the case of green solvents, ethanol, isopropanol, polyols (e.g. ethylene glycol, polyethylene glycol), and natural deep eutectic solvents (DES) (e.g., choline/amino acids [110], choline/urea [111]) were used in CDs production. Crescenzo et al. introduced an innovative method for preparing zwitterionic natural DESs from trimethylglycine and carboxylic acids that served as environmentally friendly media, facilitating the spontaneous self-assembly of gold nanoparticles [112]. Further, N-CDQs were synthesized from alkaline lignin in water, ethanol, and isopropanol under a solvothermal process [113]. The as-synthesized N-CQDs in ethanol exhibited the highest QY compared to CQDs made from other biomass sources. Ethanol is widely recognized as a green solvent due to its low toxicity and ease of recovery, making it an ideal solvent for large-scale production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conventional and recent advancements in noninvasive glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: Classification of glucose detection can be divided into two categories: electrochemical and optical techniques. Electrochemical glucose-sensing is the technique using the detection of electrochemical parameters such as utilization of electrons, bio-interaction occurrence, surface charge changing, exchange of electrons, current, or ionic conductivity changing. The electrode is mainly used to measure the current change, and the electrode is normally immobilized with an enzyme or mimetic nanozyme for analyte transformation to products that are identified via electrochemical sensors [114]. In the case of optical technique, glucose can be detected using an outstanding optical property of any fluorescent material. For example, the fluorescence intensity changes when the product is produced in the oxidation reaction of glucose or catalytic coenzyme. This causes changes in absorption or PL during the reaction, and therefore the difference in absorbance or PL can be correlated with the concentration of analytes [114]. Scheme 1 illustrated the mechanism of glucose oxidation reaction, glucose oxidase (GOx) exhibited enzymatic activity to catalyze glucose molecules to produce d-glucono-δ-lactone as the desired product while hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a by-product in the presence of oxygen. Then d-glucono-δ-lactone is hydrolyzed and turned into gluconic acid [115]. In consideration of glucose level detection, three enzymes often used based on the catalytic activity in glucose reaction are hexokinase, GOx, and glucose-1-dehydrogenase. GOx is the most standardized used for biosensing applications due to high selectivity, low price, and excellent potential against the variation of pH, temperature, and ionic strength [114].

Noninvasive techniques for glucose detection can be classified into 2 main techniques which are electrochemical and optical sensing based on different transducing elements. Electrochemical glucose-sensing is the technique using the detection of electrochemical especially the current signal change [117,118] in which the electrode is normally immobilized with artificial nanozyme for analyte transformation and identification via electrochemical sensors.

An electrochemical biosensor is a common type of device that converts biochemical reactions into electrical signals. In this sensor, the electrode plays a crucial role, serving as a solid platform for biomolecule (i.e., enzyme, antibody, and aptamer) immobilization and facilitating electron transfer. The use of various nanomaterials with large surface areas enhances the sensor's performance by increasing the loading capacity and improving the mass transport of reactants, resulting in higher analytical sensitivity. Electrochemical sensors are categorized into non-carbon nanomaterials e.g., gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), silver nanoparticles(AgNPs), platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs) , Fe3O4 NPs, and carbon-based materials including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, and graphene oxide (GO) [15]. Liu et al. developed nitrogen-doped carbon nanofibers (NCNFs) film and modified it with a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for an excellent electrochemical measurement for glucose biosensing applications [117] as shown in Fig. 8 A. Electrical current was developed when adding glucose solution into the film as shown in Fig. 8B. The result exhibited that the addition of N-doping provided a faster electron transfer rate, quick response, and high electrocatalytic properties in oxygen reduction. From Fig. 8C, the high performance of catalytic electrochemical detection was confirmed by the steady-state current that reached 95 % in only 3 s after glucose addition. Inset is the calibration curve of different glucose concentrations in a range of 0.2 to 1.2 mM showing linear response with an R2 of 0.998 and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.06 mM. These responses confirmed the efficiency of sensitive electrochemical biosensing in low glucose concentrations in the millimolar unit. In Fig. 8D, the specific current signal was raised at 50 s and 175 s after glucose was added to the reaction indicating more sensitivity of glucose detection over ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), and dopamine (DA). In another study, Zhang et al. [118] developed a nanofibrous membrane of polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) modified with multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and PtNPs for catalytic biosensing applications. This study expected the high conductivity and catalytic activity of MWCNTs after fabrication with PtNPs via an electrospinning technique. From Fig. 8E, the current increased with increasing H2O2 concentration resulted from the reduction of H2O2 in the long-testing response. The H2O2 sensitivity was also determined compared with other electroactive species including AA, UA, and DA. Clearly high selectivity of H2O2 sensing was shown in Fig. 10F while no response was observed in relevant electroactive species under potential 0.6 V.

For ultra-low glucose detection in biofluids, salivary glucose (SG) sensors were first time constructed through a multi-layer self-assembly procedure involving single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCN), chitosan, AuNPs and GOx on a screen-printed Pt electrode. SG level was measured using an electrochemical technique in both buffer solutions and saliva samples. In buffer solution, a linear range from 0.5 to 20 mg dL−1 (27.8 μM to 1.11 mM) with a LOD of 0.41 mg dL−1, and a sensitivity of 0.24 μA·s·dL·mg−1 for only 30 s period were achieved. For saliva testing of 10 healthy subjects, SG concentrations ranging from 1.1 to 10.1 mg dL−1 (61.1 μM to 0.56 mM) were effectively performed indicating that this noninvasive SG monitoring could serve as an alternative technique for diabetes self-monitoring at home [119]. In a recent progress, tear glucose was successfully monitored using TOUCH sensor which combined glucose dehydrogenases (GDHs) and a redox cofactor of flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD). Compared to GOx, GDHs have the advantage of not using oxygen as an electron acceptor, making them insensitive to variations in oxygen levels. Effective protein engineering efforts have led to the production of fungal GDH-FAD with high glucose selectivity and high catalytic activity, and it is capable of direct electron transfer. A good linear relationship between tear glucose and the current was obtained between 0.27 and 1.11 mM with an LOD of 0.04 mM [120].

Lately, Lin et al. designed a noninvasive electrochemical sensor for detecting glucose in sweat (Fig. 9F). Small quantities of secretion from natural sweating can be rapidly absorbed at the fingertips, palm, and back of the hand (Fig. 9G), where eccrine sweat glands are most concentrated. This aqueous environment facilitated the diffusion of metabolites from sweat into the hydrogels, allowing the patches to efficiently capture and collect analytes (Fig. 9H). The soft hydrogel reservoir delivered its contents to the multilayered sweat glucose sensor, which incorporated a nanostructured hybrid film with superior electrochemical performance and sensitivity. The sensor consisted of multiple layers, starting with a mediator conducting layer composed of Prussian blue (PB) and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) nanocomposites (PB-PEDOT NC) (0.62 μm), followed by a GOx immobilization layer, and topped with a protective Nafion coating (Fig. 9I). When the hydrogel interacted with the biosensor, the GOx layer generated H2O2 in direct relation to the glucose concentration in the patch. This H2O2 was then swiftly reduced by PB at a low overpotential. The resulting signal was tracked hourly to enable continuous glucose monitoring at different locations using the sweat glucose sensor (Fig. 9J). The PB efficiently reduced H₂O₂ at a low overpotential, improving the sensing process. The wearable biosensor exhibited a broad linear detection range (6.25 μM to 0.8 mM, with R² = 0.992) and provided selective glucose detection in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), reaching an LOD of 4 μM [125]. Leveraging comfort and convenience, sweat glucose sensors offer a promising approach for straightforward routine glucose management in real-world applications.

Continuous monitoring of tear fluids is often carried out using electronic glucose sensors; however, the metal sensors are generally not biocompatible, restricting their use for in vivo measurements. Additionally, the sensor response is prone to electrical drifts, necessitating frequent recalibration with fresh blood samples. Furthermore, drawbacks such as the bulky detector, complex fabrication process, and numerous electrical components in the electronic glucose system highlight the need for significant improvements in both device design and the overall measurement approach [126]. According to the electrochemical properties, an increase in deposition charge leads to varied surface morphologies at the nanocomposite interface, which in turn enhances both electrochemical activity and charge transfer resistance. This variation would also influence the effectiveness of electrochemical enzymatic glucose measurements. Therefore, the challenge is to enhance the surface characteristics of the nanocomposite electrode that significantly provides exceptional stability and electrocatalytic activity to improve noninvasive biofluid glucose measurement.

For green fabrication methods of electrochemical glucose sensors, using reducing/oxidizing chemical reagents for metal nanoparticle synthesis for electrochemical electrodes for glucose detection such as sodium borohydride (NaBH4) leads to affect eco-friendliness and health risks [15]. Therefore, more environmentally friendly reagents potentially replace the aforementioned toxic reagents, such as citrate, ascorbate, enzymes, extracts from plants, microbial, and fungi. These bioactive reducing agents are alternative options [82]. Tea polyphenols are large molecules characterized as aromatic rings that feature hydroxyl groups as substituents. These hydroxyl groups function as strong ligands capable of reducing hard metals (like Fe3+) and intermediate/soft metals (such as Fe2+), while the carbonyl groups served as soft ligands and can coordinate with zero-valent iron [127]. As a result, the polyphenolic structure exhibits two key properties: it can reduce oxidation states and offers electrosteric stabilization. To fabricate the sensor with greener methods, the introduction of conductive organic polymers such as poly (3-hexythiophene) (P3HT) organic thin film transistor (OTFT), carbon nanotube field effect transistor (FETs) [128], and organic transistors such as N-type conducting polymer poly (benzimidazobenzophenanthroline) (BBL) [129], poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): poly (styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) film [130], have been reported recently for electrochemical glucose sensing. To replace synthetic polymeric conductive film, Elkington et al. reported the use of P3HT OTFT to detect glucose concentrations in saliva [131]. OTFTs are particularly suitable for biosensing applications due to the compatibility of the materials with biological recognition elements like enzymes. In contrast, traditional electronic materials such as silicon and metals require high processing temperatures and are non-porous, making them unsuitable for enzyme integration, as these enzymes can denature at elevated temperatures and need direct contact with their target analytes. Moreover, the cost-effective fabrication of organic electronic materials enhances their usability in disposable applications where sensors are intended for single use. While organic semiconductors, such as P3HT, may have limitations like lower charge carrier mobility—hindering their performance in high-speed applications—this is less critical for many sensing applications, where it is acceptable for detection results to take tens or even hundreds of seconds. Additionally, organic semiconductors can be easily modified by adding a thin interfacial layer to immobilize biological recognition elements for OTFT-based sensors [132].

Glucose can be detected using an outstanding optical property of fluorescent materials. For example, the fluorescence intensity changes when the product is produced in the oxidation reaction of glucose, catalytic coenzyme, or the color change of indicator due to radical species that cause the oxidation of that indicating substrate. This causes changes in UV–vis absorption or PL during the reaction, and therefore they can be correlated with concentration of analytes [114].

Artificial enzymatic reactions catalyzed by CD-based nanocatalysts have been inspired in living organisms. Notably, the excellent water dispersibility of CDs, due to their abundant hydrophilic surface groups, makes them a highly promising nanomaterial for nanozyme applications. Recent studies have also revealed, for the first time, for the significant role of peroxidases in driving angiogenesis, particularly concerning cancer development [133]. CDs display peroxidase (POD)-like activity when exposed to H₂O₂, indicated by a color change resulting from the oxidation of various chromogenic substrates. These chromogenic substrates include 2,2′-azino-bis-3-ethylbenzothiozoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS), 2,3-trihydroxybenzene (THB), hydroquinone, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), pyrogallol, and o-phenylenediamine (OPD). TMB is the most common substrate for studying horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and other peroxidases (POD) mimetic activity owing to its low toxicity, carcinogenicity, and high UV–vis absorbance [134–136]. PODs can catalyze H2O2 to produce •OH radicals, which further oxidize colorless TMB to blue oxidized TMB (ox-TMB).

Initially, Zheng et al. found that CDs derived from candle soot exhibited peroxidase-like activity in oxidizing THB, OPD, and TMB in the presence of H₂O₂, as evidenced by the color change of ox-TMB. In contrast to HRP, these CDs retain stable catalytic activity over a broad range of pH (pH 2–12) and temperature (0–90 °C). CDs moreover were found to obey a ping-pong mechanism similar to HRP, and exhibit higher affinities for POD substrates and H2O2 expressed by enzymatic kinetic parameter, Km and Vm[137]. Non-toxic metal-doped CDs such as Ag, Pt, and Au were found to enhance peroxidase-like reactivity using TMB reagent in the presence of H2O2, resulting in increasing Vm, and reducing considerably Km [89]. Leveraging the combined POD-like activity of CDs and GOx activity, a cost-effective, straightforward, selective, and sensitive assay was developed for colorimetric glucose detection in serum samples. In a previous study, the synthesis of Fe-metal-organic framework (Fe-MOF) immobilized with GOx for colorimetric detection of glucose using TMB as a substrate was revealed (Fig. 10 A). Successfully loading GOx on the structure of Fe-MOF was investigated by covalent bond formation with crosslinked agents. GOx in integrated enzyme Fe-MOF-GOx complex firstly catalyzed the glucose oxidation reaction to generate gluconic acid and H2O2 in the presence of O2. Then, H2O2 was oxidized by Fe-MOF-GOx into the hydroxyl radicals (·OH), which caused the oxidation of TMB to generate ox-TMB (blue color) observed by UV–vis at a wavelength of 652 nm as shown in Fig. 10B [138]. The Fe-MOF-GOx showed a significantly increased UV–vis absorbance at 652 nm and the blue-colored occurrence when the concentration of glucose increases from 0 up to 500 µM providing the linear relationship with a high R2 of 0.990 with a low LOD of 0.478 μM as shown in Fig. 10C [138]. This work provided a simple, visual, and effective colorimetric assay for glucose detection in human serum.

Palladium-ruthenium aerogels co-doped with boron and nitrogen (B, N-PdRu aerogels) were synthesized utilizing ammonia borane (NH3BH3) as a reducing agent. Their distinctive porous structure and the synergistic effects of the doped atoms contributed to an increased peroxidase-mimicking activity. In the presence of H2O2, these aerogels efficiently catalyzed the oxidation of TMB, producing a blue solution with an optimal absorption wavelength of 652 nm. Consequently, when combined with the GOx enzyme in a tandem reaction, the B, N-PdRu aerogels can be utilized for sensitive glucose determination. This new method provides a good linear detection range for glucose from 10 μM to 2 mM, with an LOD as low as 6 μM. In the human serum test conducted within the glucose range of 4.35–6.7 mM, the analytical results obtained were closely aligned with those from the glucometer, exhibiting relative differences of less than 6%. This indicates a significant potential for practical blood glucose detection. Furthermore, these findings suggest considerable promise for applications in disease diagnosis [139].

In recent progress on ultra-low glucose detection through colorimetric assay, CDs-based nanozymes have not been established, and most of them were enzymatic-based nanocomposites. A study revealed the application of a chitosan sponge for sampling artificial saliva, attributed to its superior absorption capacity and its ability to release the retained saliva. Concurrently, a sensing module comprising a POD immobilized polyethylene glycol-based hydrogel (POD/GOx-PEG) was developed, in which encapsulated enzymes and dyes was conducted for the colorimetric detection of target biomarkers. Upon insertion of the saliva-absorbed chitosan sponge into the loading zone and subsequent compression, the retained saliva was discharged and transferred to the detection zone, which housed the hydrogel-based sensing module. This initiated an enzyme-catalyzed reaction, resulting in a color change in the hydrogel. Given that salivary glucose levels are typically 1.18 and 1.23 mg dL-1 (or 65.6 μM and 68.3 μM) in healthy individuals, and 4.95 and 4.22 mg dL-1 (or 0.28 mM and 0.23 mM) in diabetic patients, the glucose concentration was successfully monitored within the range of 0.5–5 mg dL-1 or 27.8 μM to 0.28 mM (R2 = 0.9957) and LOD of 0.5 mg dL-1 or 27.8 μM glucose [140]. Just recently, HRP/GOx/alginate beads were fabricated for colorimetric detection of glucose in sweat at very low concentrations in a microfluidic device. A perfect linearity of glucose in a range of 10–100 µM with R2 = 0.977 in green value at 13 min with an LOD of 3.8 μM was achieved [141].

To enhance the practical measurement of tear glucose concentration, Jeon and coworkers developed a novel, clinically viable optical quantification technique utilizing nanoparticle-embedded contact lenses (NECLs) as demonstrated in Fig. 10D. The NECLs' colorimetric contact lens consists of cerium oxide nanoparticles (CNPs), GOx and a PEG complex. When the NECLs oxidize glucose in tears, they generate H₂O₂, which then reacts with Ce³⁺, leading to a yellow color change as Ce³⁺ is converted to Ce⁴⁺ (Fig. 10E). The color variation of the NECL at low glucose concentrations (0.001, 0.01, and 0.1 mM) was investigated with a color CCD camera (Fig. 10F). A marked change in colorimetric response was recorded at 0.1 mM, as indicated by the optical monitoring system (p < 0.01 relative to 0 mM). The linear regression between a low glucose concentration range of 0.1–0.6 mM, with intervals of 0.1 mM was created as a standard curve. The two linear regression lines closely overlapped, with the mean difference in estimated glucose concentration being ±0.005 mM across 50 trials [126]. The assessment of the NECL biosensor for measuring glucose in tears was effectively confirmed through experiments involving both human tear samples and in vivo, in situ animal models. This method represents a notable step forward in creating a fully automated system for continuous monitoring of glucose levels in tears for patients with diabetes. The results have expanded the use of CNP-PEG-GOx-loaded contact lenses to include a smartphone-based image-processing algorithm for noninvasive glucose level monitoring in human tears. Glucose concentrations in tear samples from both diabetic and nondiabetic human volunteers were assessed using these contact lenses alongside a glucose assay kit. The glucose concentrations in tears measured with the CNP-PEG-GOx-loaded contact lenses were 1.0 ± 0.1 mM for diabetic volunteers and 0.5 ± 0.1 mM for nondiabetic volunteers. In contrast, using the glucose assay kit, the tear glucose concentrations were found to be 0.90 ± 0.17 mM for diabetic volunteers and 0.30 ± 0.06 mM for nondiabetic volunteers [142]. In the clinical trial, creating GMP-grade contact lenses integrated with the CNP-PEG-GOx nanocomplex and developing a compensation algorithm utilizing a deep-learning application pose significant challenges. This will enable the system to adjust to various environmental factors, including indoor and outdoor conditions, changes in weather (sunny or cloudy), and fluctuations in temperature (hot or cold).

Various nanomaterials were developed for optical sensing, for example, noble metals-based carbon NPs [143], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [144], metal chalcogenide (MC) and transition MCs [145], MOFs [146], etc. Optical glucose sensing can be performed by the catalytic ability of GOx which catalyzes the oxidation of glucose to produce gluconic acid as a desired product and H2O2 as a by-product. After H2O2 is generated, the nanozyme acts as a peroxidase to catalyze not only the decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2, but also produce OH radicals (·OH) which is a key substrate for color change in later peroxidase-like activity. This reaction with TMB leads to the optical detection of glucose [147]. Peroxidase-mimetic nanomaterial can catalyze H2O2 to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can transform the colorless substrates to colored-product [143,148], thus H2O2 can be quantified using the colorimetric method. The absorbance of the color in the mixture solution is determined when TMB is oxidized by ·OH from H2O2 dissociation catalyzed by POD enzyme or nanocatalyst, so-called the peroxidase-like reaction. Hence, for glucose sensing, glucose catalyzed by GOx can be converted to gluconic acid and H2O2, H2O2 is further detected by POD-like reaction [149]. It was reported on fluorometric sensing of glucose and H2O2 using fluorescence sensing which turns on/turns off the fluorescence property of nanomaterial due to the H2O2 reaction toward nanomaterials’ surface is very sensitive at ultra-low glucose level.

Lately, fluorescence approaches for glucose sensing under visible-to-near infrared light irradiation have been extensively developed according to the advantages in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, and non-invasive measurement. Fluorescence techniques have very little to no effect on the host system and are extremely sensitive to the target molecule. A variety of fluorescence sensing strategies have been proposed such as fluorescence quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), turn on-off or turn off-on anisotropy (FA), ratiometric, metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF), and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [150]. One of the most cited and exceptional reports on fluorescence glucose sensing was using boronic acid-modified CDs as a nanoprobe. This resulted in a linear correlation between fluorescence quenching and glucose concentration in the range of 9–900 μM with a low LOD of 1.5 μM. This CDs-based fluorescence nanosensor needs no costly enzymes and effectively quantifies glucose with a higher sensitivity 10–250 times relative to previous fluorescent nanosensing systems [151]. Recently, a simple and cost-effective method was proposed to fabricate CDs supported on AgNPs as a fluorescent nanoprobe for glucose detection as shown in Fig. 11 (B) [152]. After the complexation of CDs/AgNPs, the blue fluorescence of CDs was quenched by surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer (SPEET) from CDs. During glucose oxidation, H2O2 released from the reaction splits AgNPs to Ag ions, liberating CDs and thus recovering the blue fluorescence property of CDs at 530 nm under excitation at 420 nm. The linear correlation between glucose concentration and fluorescence intensity was obtained with R 2 of 0.9992 in a range of 2–100 μM glucose with an LOD of 1.39 μM [152]. The ratiometric fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose by Fe-doped CDs has been freshly reported by Zhu et al. [153]. Fe-CDs based ratiometric fluorescence sensing system was designed according to the change of fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs and 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) which was the oxidative product of OPD at 460 nm and 560 nm, respectively. The DAP fluorescence intensity ratio of I560/I460 was proportionally correlated with the glucose concentrations in the range of 0–300 μM with an LOD of 2.5 μM.

Ma et al. reported the synthesis of carbon nanodots supported on silver nanoparticles (CDs/AgNPs) as a glucose biosensor with a fluorescence quenching technique [152]. This nanocomposite was designed using the unique properties of CDs in an optical analysis. Fig. 11A depicts a mechanism of CDs/AgNPs with glucose solution which resulted in fluorescence quenching output. First, the Ag ions reacted with free fluorescent CDs to form CDs/AgNPs, leading to decreased fluorescence of general CDs. In the presence of H2O2, CDs/AgNPs nanocomposite were suddenly disintegrated and the released CDs exhibited strong fluorescence once again. As shown in Fig. 11B, the UV–vis absorption peak of CDs/AgNPs was detected around 380 nm, after adding H2O2 this peak disappeared and CDs demonstrated UV–vis excitation spectrum at a maximum peak around 420 nm. Due to the SPEET of nanocomposites from CDs to AgNPs - donor to acceptor, fluorescence quenching in the presence of AgNPs was shown in Fig. 11C. For glucose biosensing, GOx catalyzes the oxidation of glucose, then gluconic acid and H2O2)are generated. Afterward, the structure of CDs/AgNPs was destroyed by H2O2 which was the by-product of glucose oxidation which led to freeing CDs from Ag ions and restored the CD's fluorescence. Therefore, the increased fluorescence response with increasing concentration of H2O2 was directly related to the concentration of glucose. Fig. 11B showed CDs/AgNPs (red line at 390 nm) with blue-shifted UV–vis absorption spectrum compared with only CDs solution (green line at 410 nm). The changing of fluorescence intensities before (green line) and after (blue line) adding H2O2 was observed in Fig. 11C. CDs exhibited strong fluorescence emission (red line) while conjugation with AgNPs caused quenching of fluorescence property (green line). The fluorescence intensities were enhanced after H2O2 was added due to etched Ag ions and the reoccurrence of freeing CDs. This can conclude the ability of H2O2 sensing of CDs/AgNPs whose fluorescence intensity was correlated directly with H2O2 concentration. The more freeing CDs released in the H2O2 etching of CDs/AgNPs step, the increasing fluorescence intensities were obtained [152].

In another work, the a-GQDs were synthesized from fructose and aniline using a one-pot microwave-assisted method through microwave heating (Fig. 11D). The GQDs were formed via inter- and intra-molecular dehydrative condensation. Alongside, aniline reacted with the carboxyl and carbonyl groups at the GQDs’ edges, introducing aniline functionality to the a-GQDs. The UV–vis absorption peak of GQDs at 257 nm shifts to 290 nm demonstrating an absorption peak at 328 nm for a2-GQDs corresponding to n-π* electronic transitions related to various surface passivation, since the −COOH groups were converted to OCNH bonds via aniline functionalization. Fig. 11H shows the FL spectra for GQDs and a-GQDs at different aniline concentrations (10 mg, 20 mg, and 30 mg) under UV excitation at 365 nm. As aniline functionalization increases, the emission peak of a-GQDs markedly red-shifts from 429 to 470 nm supported by the FL color change under UV light (inset of Fig. 11E). Moreover, a high fluorescence QY of 38% from a-GQDs underscores their potential for biosensing and bioimaging applications. The aqueous solution of a-GQDs was utilized as ink and printed onto filter paper which is not easily detectable under daylight but exhibits a uniform bright FL when exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 11Fa). After phenyl-boric acid (PBA) dropwise onto the a-CQDs printed device, the FL was quenched with the a-GQDs/PBA system (Fig. 11Fb). However, when different concentrations of glucose were added, the fluorescence of the device displayed a distinctly brighter blue color as the glucose concentration rose. The recycling test was successfully conducted over ten consecutive cycles using 10 mM glucose after ethanol rinsing (Fig. 11Fc). A good linear correlation between FL and glucose concentration was found between 0 and 40 mM (R2 = 0.999) (Fig. 11G) and the LOD of FL difference from the glucose blank was 0.1 mM [154].

Duan et al. [38] reported on chemiluminescence (CL) glucose sensing using copper (Cu) doping on CDs. Modified CD surface with luminol for enhancing its optical properties was considered due to its stimulated ability with related anion which resulted in strong CL intensity. The enzyme-like activity of Cu-doped CDs was investigated in Fig. 12 A. The enhancement of CL intensity of H2O2 with luminol on Cu-doped CDs (blue line) was proposed. This reaction started with the luminol molecule which generated luminol anion under alkaline conditions. Added H2O2 is stimulated by Cu-doped CDs to produce OH radicals and superoxide radicals. Then, a chemical reaction occurred on the Cu-doped CD surface led to electron exchange that resulted in enhancing strong CL emission intensity. After the oxidation of luminol, the CL emission peak increased (blue line) due to the chemical reaction of Cu-CDs-luminol anion-superoxide radicals compared with only luminol reacted on Cu-doped CDs surface (red line). In contrast, H2O2 reacted with luminol molecule and no peak was observed (black line). The results confirmed the catalytic effect of Cu-doped CDs with enhancement of CL sensing properties. CL spectra of Cu-doped CDs in different glucose concentrations were analyzed in Fig. 12B. The increase of CL intensities at 420 nm with increasing glucose concentrations was found. Fig. 12C demonstrated a linear correlation between CL intensities at 420 nm and glucose concentrations ranging from 1.0 to 48.0 µM. The linear equation was used to calculate the LOD of glucose showing a low-level glucose detection at 0.32 µM [38].

According to metal ions used as the catalyst in the reaction, co-enzyme was also selected to create new ideas of nanocomposites for glucose biosensing. Enzyme is known as a biological catalyst due to their properties in accelerating the reaction rate, high stability, and specificity. In many researches on the development of glucose sensing material, enzyme was used to generate OH radicals which are important substances that further catalyze the oxidation of glucose for the glucose detection assay. The glucose sensing assay was not only in the observation of optical properties but also in the alteration of physical and chemical properties of synthesized nanocomposite. Park and Park [155] reported a fluorescent sensitive technique of poly(acrylic acid) (PAAc) hydrogel droplets with CDs immobilized GOx and HRP as enzymatic reaction assistors. The reaction of bi-enzymes including GOx and HRP with glucose leads to gluconic acid and H2O2 generation. The generated H2O2 is further dissociated to OH radicals catalyzed by HRP. The results of •OH radicals on CD oxidation showed CD fluorescence quenching, while gluconic acid caused hydrogel droplet size reduction. Fig. 12D exhibited PL spectra of CDs/PAAc with the highest peak at 450 nm, and the intensities decreased after glucose was added with the time increased. Fig. 12E depicted an increased degree of fluorescence quenching (Qf) of synthesized CDs/hydrogel droplets which was almost stable after 5 h in glucose solution. Qf was calculated as the following equation; Qf = (F-F0)/F0 where
F ,
F 0
are the PL intensities at 450 nm with and without glucose, respectively. On the other hand, other properties which are the size of the hydrogel and pH of the solution decreased in glucose solution. The size of droplets was extremely dropped and continuously reduced until ∼ 5 h. The pH of the aqueous solution decreased from pH 7.0 to 4.2 due to the gluconic acid generation.

The main focus of this work was to observe the optical characteristic change of CD@GOx@HRP when adding bi-enzyme in the glucose reaction via fluorescence imaging in Fig. 12F. PAAc hydrogel droplets (i) with only CD addition and (iii) with CD@GOx@HRP which is an enzymatic reaction model showed similar light blue fluorescence and size of droplets before glucose solution was added. After adding glucose solution, hydrogel droplets (iv) with CD@GOx@HRP showed significant fluorescence quenching and size reduction, while (ii) hydrogel with only CD exhibited no fluorescence change. This observation was described by the reaction between enzymes and glucose solution. As shown in Fig. 12G, the reaction of GOx with glucose produces gluconic acid and H2O2 as products. The generated H2O2 is catalyzed by the HRP enzyme and is decomposed to OH radicals which cause the fluorescence quenching characteristic of CDs. From this correlative mechanism, Qf is applied as an indicator to evaluate the amount of glucose in the solution [155]. The sensitivity of glucose detection compared with other substances in human body was discovered by Qf. Higher Qf of both glucose and glucose+dopamine relative to urea, ascorbic acid, dopamine, lactose, and NaCl confirmed good selectivity of GOx enzyme with a glucose molecule. Significant fluorescence quenching image and size reduction of hydrogel droplets when adding glucose were depicted and image analysis was performed [155].

A study on GOx immobilized onto CDs for ultralow glucose detection in model solution was revealed using a ratiometric fluorescence assay. A nanocomposite between CDs and rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) was used for assays both in a solution form and in a poly(acrylic acid) crosslinked film. A ratiometric fluorescence quenching and color change from blue to green at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm was observed when glucose concentration increased owing to the reaction of GOx toward glucose, followed by HRP toward H2O2 generated. The optimized aqueous CD/Rh6G/GOx/HRP solution exhibited a linear detection range of 0.1–500 μM with an LOD of 0.04 μM. For the test in human blood serum, the system showed good selectivity for glucose over other major blood components. In case of immobilized CD/Rh6G/GOx/HRP hydrogel film, using ratiometric fluorescence color change, similar sensitivity with a linear range of 0.5–500 μM and an LOD of 0.08 μM glucose were obtained [156]. In another study, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was used as a matrix to synthesize PVP-stabilized Pt-Ru nanozymes which showed strong peroxidase-like activity for colorimetric and fluorometric glucose determination through a cascade reaction with GOx. The detectable range of colorimetric glucose detection was 0.25–3.0 mM, with an R² of 0.988 and an LOD of 138 μM. To study the peroxidase activity of the nanozymes through fluorescent signals, Amplex Red (AR) was used as a substrate. The oxidized form of AR, resorufin, is excited at 563 nm and emits fluorescence at 587 nm (FL563/587). A linear relationship between glucose concentration and increased FL563/587 intensity was observed between 5.0 and 300 μM glucose (R² = 0.997), with an LOD of 1.11 μM. The fluorometric assay showed greater sensitivity and a lower LOD for glucose sensing compared to the colorimetric assay. Moreover, the PVP/PtRu nanozymes (NZs) demonstrated high stability for over a month of storage and achieved excellent recovery values in human serum and artificial urine, ranging from 94 % to 106 % [74].

Saliva is an attractive alternative biological medium for clinical analyses due to its diverse range of clinically relevant biomarkers and compounds. A study explored a straightforward, rapid, and cost-effective pretreatment method for saliva samples using a cotton-paper-syringe filtration system, facilitating the analysis of saliva samples with multilayer paper devices. Fig. 12H demonstrated the saliva sampling by (a) inserting a cotton ball into the patient's mouth, (b) placing it into a syringe, and (c) filtering through the filter while Fig. 12H(d) illustrated the glucose detection by measuring the H2O2 generated in the presence of GOx. In bulk solution, these reactions require sufficient O2 to oxidize glucose, making the availability of O2 a key limiting factor. However, paper-based platforms (CD/GOx/HRP-3D-multiple paper) possess a large surface area and are exposed to air, thus eliminating the O2 limitation, and thus the analysis time was reduced. In the absence of H2O2, the CDs emitted blue light under UV light, but the fluorescence intensity decreased in the presence of H2O2 and HRP, allowing the quantification of glucose by fluorescence quenching (Fig. 12H(d)). This quenching effect was due to the hydroxyl groups formed during the HRP-catalyzed reduction of H2O2, which caused crosslinking of the phenolic groups on the CD surface. The glucose concentration showed good linear correlations with the fluorescence quenching, with LODs of 2.60 μM [157].

The most recent progress in noninvasive, and wearable optical techniques for ultralow glucose detection were found in sweat monitoring. A groundbreaking fluorescent nanosensor combines boric acid (BA)-functionalized CQDs that are doped with heteroatoms (BA/CQD) and is designed for use in paper-based analytical devices. This configuration, along with a microfluidic channel made from hydrophilic cotton thread, results in a flexible, wearable sensor for sweat that is biocompatible and biodegradable. It allows for the real-time monitoring of glucose levels in sweat, which can be displayed on a smartphone [158]. From Fig. 13 A, glucose analysis using the Stern–Volmer plot was performed when the green percentage was derived from the acquired RGB images at different pHs. A good linear correlation between GF0/GF and glucose concentration ranging from 1 μM to 1 mM (R2 ≥ 0.97) with an LOD of 1 μM was achieved for all pHs when GF and GF0 are the fluorescence intensities of CQDs with and without glucose, respectively. An increase in pH to 7.5 resulted in higher fluorescence intensity, leading to a greater Stern–Volmer quenching constant when analyzing glucose concentration against emission intensity. This effect is likely attributed to dipole interactions and the adsorption of glucose onto functionalized CQDs due to the presence of various surface groups, including –OH, −BO, −BOH, −OCO, −COC, −NNN, and NO, as confirmed by FTIR analysis. A smartphone was utilized to analyze the collected sweat and quantitatively determine glucose levels (Fig. 13B) under a UVC tube (11 W, 254 nm). For non-diabetic volunteers, glucose concentrations ranged from 14.89 ± 0.86 μM to 15.94 ± 0.75 μM, whereas for diabetic volunteers, glucose concentrations ranged from 27.76 ± 0.89 μM to 49.75 ± 0.98 μM.

Another promising fluorescence imaging for ultrasensitive glucose detection in sweat utilized CDs as a fluorescent probe. The blue fluorescence emitted by CDs was reduced through a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process involving porous silicon (PSi) and CDs. As a result, the skin pad initially showed red fluorescence from the PSi. When the skin pad was placed on the body, H2O2 produced by the oxidation of sweat glucose catalyzed by GOx induced the oxidation of PSi, causing it to lose fluorescence and the CDs to regain it, thus changing the color from red at λEm ∼ 700 nm (PSi) to blue at λEm ∼ 500 nm (CDs) (Fig. 13C). This visible FL shift from red to blue was related to the amount of sweat glucose existing (Fig. 13D). Bimetallic Au and Ag assisted in fastening the FL change from red to blue and green within 1–2 h instead of 8 h. A wearable skin pad can be easily created by co-immobilizing bimetallic AuNPs and AgNPs with CDs and PSi (BiM-CDs/PSi), along with GOx, onto a transparent, biocompatible chitosan film that was backed by a sticky polyurethane membrane. Sweat glucose levels were monitored over 10 h while participants slept. This study involved 8 diabetic individuals and 16 healthy volunteers. A BiM-CQDs@PSi/GOX/CS/PU pad was attached to the back of the neck. To identify hypoglycemia or normal blood glucose (BG) levels, an image from a volunteer with a BG of 6.0 mM, close to the critical threshold of 6.2 mM for hypoglycemia, was used as the reference color. Healthy individuals displayed predominantly red images, indicating normal BG levels. The visual results for detecting hyperglycemia aligned with measurements from a commercial blood glucometer, suggesting the method's potential for non-invasive and semi-quantitative physiological glucose monitoring. The proposed process for noninvasive and visual monitoring of sweat glucose for preventive care at home is illustrated in Fig. 13E. The findings indicate that using this sensing technology, clinical tests conducted on diabetics and healthy volunteers can effectively identify hyperglycemia [159].

The correlation between the concentration of glucose with the intensity of signals for each method called the linear range of detected glucose and the LOD which showed the lowest amount of detected glucose in each technique waere demonstrated in Table 2. The different types of glucose detection studies, classified into 2 main techniques based on transducing elements, are electrochemical and optical sensing including chemiluminescence, colorimetry, and fluorometry including fluorescent imaging. The reported linear range is generally focused on the low glucose concentrations that are mostly close to the range of fasting biofluids glucose levels between 0.05–0.55 mM and 0.55–1.77 mM for normal and diabetic patients, respectively. Most of the optical techniques reported at low range of glucose concentrations and the LOD values to micromolar unit providing information for sensitive glucose detection in biofluids such as sweat, tears and saliva.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of greenness and functionality of analytical methods for glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: The assessment of greenness and functionality including analytical and practical criteria of glucose detection assays among electrochemical, colorimetric, and fluorometric methods in the review regarding key criteria such as reagent toxicity, waste production, energy consumption, and user safety is critically discussed as follows.

Reagent Toxicity : Electrochemical sensors often employ enzyme-based systems (e.g., GOx or GDH) immobilized on electrodes. While enzymes are generally non-toxic, the electrodes themselves may contain materials such as gold, platinum, silver, or palladium, which could be environmentally unfriendly depending on how they are processed or disposed of. Recent trends are exploring greener alternatives like carbon-based electrodes, which reduce the reliance on toxic heavy metals. Fluorescence assays often use fluorophores, some of which may be toxic or harmful to the environment. These chemical dyes or nanoparticles can pose risks if mishandled or improperly disposed of. There has been progress toward greener alternatives, like environmentally friendly CDs, CQDs, or GQDs as fluorescent probes, though these are still not widespread in practice. A green synthesis method of carbon nanomaterial from natural sources with nontoxicity solvents such as water or organic solvents like ethanol [42], and polyethylene glycol [43] has been widely studied. Colorimetric sensors often use chemical agents that undergo a visible color change in response to glucose. These agents can sometimes be toxic (e.g., chromophores or dyes). Greener alternatives are being researched, such as using natural or organic pigments, but they often lack the sensitivity of traditional agents.

Waste Production : Electrochemical sensors are known for generating minimal waste. Many designs are reusable or can be miniaturized into disposable systems that use small volumes of reagents, reducing both chemical waste and environmental impact. However, the disposal of sensors with heavy metal electrodes could be problematic if not properly managed. The conductive organic conductive polymers such as poly (3-hexythiophene) (P3HT) organic thin film transistor (OTFT), carbon nanotube field effect transistor (FETs) [128], and organic transistors such as N-type conducting polymer poly (benzimidazobenzophenanthroline) (BBL) [129], poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): poly (styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) film [130], have been reported recently for electrochemical glucose sensing. Fluorescent sensors can generate significant waste, primarily from the use of chemical reagents and solvents in sample preparation and cleaning. The disposal of fluorophores and used reagents can also contribute to environmental burdens. Miniaturization and microfluidics offer a potential reduction in reagent use, but this is still under development [160]. Colorimetric assays typically involve one-time use of reagents, which leads to significant waste, especially if large sample volumes are required. The waste also depends on how efficiently the color-changing agents are used and disposed of. Fluorometric or colorimetric sensing from biodegradable polymer-based microneedles or paper-based devices with an increased cycle of use could help to reduce waste production [154].

Energy Consumption : Electrochemical sensors typically require low energy for operation, especially when integrated into portable or wearable devices. The preparation steps are often minimal, requiring no sophisticated instruments. This makes them one of the most energy-efficient options. Fluorescence assays require energy-intensive equipment, such as light sources (e.g., lasers, LEDs) and sensitive detectors. This makes them less energy-efficient compared to electrochemical methods. Continuous monitoring systems, in particular, can consume large amounts of energy over extended periods. Colorimetric sensors are relatively low-energy in operation. Detection can often be achieved with simple visual inspection or basic optical devices, making them more accessible for low-energy, low-cost applications. However, certain forms of detection (such as using spectrophotometers) may require more energy than simple visual readings. Therefore, recent progress in the optical sensing device of glucose is more or less based on the color change of sample pads or contact lenses [126]. Smartphone-based sensing has been intensively proposed for both fluorometric [161–163] and colorimetric sensing [142,164,165].

User Safety : Electrochemical methods generally pose low risks to users. With minimal exposure to hazardous chemicals and ease of use, they are ideal for non-invasive monitoring and consumer applications like glucose monitoring for diabetics. There is a potential safety risk due to the use of toxic fluorophores, especially in large-scale or long-term applications. Additionally, the use of high-energy light sources could pose hazards, especially if safety protocols are not followed. Colorimetric methods tend to pose moderate risks to users due to the potential toxicity of dyes. However, if handled properly, they are generally safer than fluorescence-based methods, especially if safer reagents are used.

Functionality : Electrochemical sensors offer high sensitivity, selectivity, and rapid response times, which makes them well-suited for real-time, point-of-care glucose monitoring. Their versatility, combined with low energy requirements and minimal waste production, gives them a strong balance between greenness and functionality. Fluorescence-based sensors are highly sensitive and selective, often achieving ultrasensitive detection of glucose in low concentrations. However, the need for sophisticated equipment and reagent handling makes them less practical for portable or low-resource settings. Due to their high analytical performance, researchers have been searching for alternative approaches such as smartphone-based devices to trade off in terms of energy and reagent toxicity limit to improve their overall sustainability. While colorimetric sensors are straightforward and user-friendly, their sensitivity and selectivity can be lower than electrochemical or fluorescence methods. They are practical for simple, low-cost, and rapid detection, but may not be suitable for applications requiring high sensitivity or continuous monitoring.

Balancing Greenness with functionality : Electrochemical sensors provide the best balance between environmental sustainability and functionality. With low toxicity, minimal waste, and high sensitivity, they are ideal for continuous glucose monitoring in various settings, including consumer health products. Their minimal energy requirements and ease of use make them practical while maintaining excellent analytical performance. For fluorescence sensors, while they offer superior sensitivity and selectivity, fluorescence assays struggle to maintain a balance between greenness and functionality. High energy consumption, reagent toxicity, and waste production limit their sustainability. However, they are excellent for highly specific, research-intensive applications where performance is prioritized over environmental concerns. In the case of colorimetric sensors, these methods excel in simplicity, cost-efficiency, and ease of use, making them attractive for low-resource settings. However, they face significant challenges in sensitivity and environmental sustainability due to the waste production and potential toxicity of reagents. Greener alternatives and innovations are needed to improve both their functionality and sustainability.

A summarized comprehensive assessment of reagent toxicity, waste production, energy consumption, user safety, analytical performance, practicality, durability and reusability, and greenness of electrochemical, fluorescence, and colorimetric sensors for glucose detection in biofluids is demonstrated in Table 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concluding remarks and the way forward
ce:section.ce:para: Various analytical techniques are currently utilized for glucose detection, including electrochemistry, colorimetry, fluorometry, photoelectrochemistry, and mass spectrometry. Although, electrochemical sensors provide the best balance between environmental sustainability and functionality, among all assays, optical sensing including fluorometry and colorimetry has been claimed as a promising assay that could deliver visualized results of glucose detection and is possibly developed for point-of-care (POC) testing using visual-based or smartphone-based devices. Colorimetric assay is simpler, environmentally benign, and safe for users, however, their lower sensitivity and high detection limit are challenges. Fluorescence assays, in particular, offer advantages such as rapid reaction times, high sensitivity, excellent specificity, affordability, and straightforward operation. Most fluorescence sensors typically employ a single fluorescent probe, with its emission wavelength being highly sensitive to variations in excitation light intensity, probe concentration, and external interference. Carbon dot-based fluorescence probes for ratiometric fluorescent systems have been widely investigated most recently due to their biocompatibility, photonic stability, tunable excitation, and emission color along with efficient functionalization with related enzymes or proteins. Methods based on carbon quantum dots fluorescence that allow for immediate visual detection of analytes when excited under near visible to near infrared light which is harmless to living creatures have attracted considerable interest. These techniques rely on changes in the intensity of emitted light, producing different color shades that facilitate detection by sight. Such colorimetric approaches can yield qualitative or semi-quantitative results in the absence of sophisticated equipment, making them ideal for field measurements and POC testing, especially in settings with limited medical resources. Furthermore, combining these detection methods with smartphones enhances their functionality, resulting in user-friendly POC devices. With their portability, superior imaging capabilities, and ability to support multiple applications, smartphones are well-suited as intelligent detection platforms. Additionally, smartphone-assisted visual detection can provide improved sensitivity and accuracy compared to unaided visual assessment, enabling precise quantitative tracking of numerous biomarkers.

Recent progress in fluorescence imaging using smartphones for glucose sensing in sweat, tears, or saliva has been considered an alternative approach for point-of-care testing for human metabolism for maintaining good health and well-being. The challenge is further complicated by other crucial factors such as secretion/reabsorption processes, sweat or tear rate, skin or tissue contamination, sebum secretions, and sweat, saliva, or tear gland byproducts, all of which influence the final composition of biofluids. This review has demonstrated significant advancements in materials, characteristics, and microsystem designs which are typically published in journals that emphasize material science and engineering as well as sensor technology. However, there is a scarcity of research discussing biofluids composition from biochemical and physiological viewpoints. Therefore, future studies should aim to address these aspects of glucose sensing in biofluids i.e., sweat, tears, saliva, etc. The overall balance between greenness and functionality, with low reagent toxicity, minimal waste, and high sensitivity of glucose sensing techniques was in detail discussed in the review. This comparison highlights the need for continued innovation to optimize both performance and sustainability in glucose detection technologies.
